item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. I love a good weird exploration, and though nothing exceedingly weird actually turned up, you did a good job of keeping what's mostly a series of doors and hallways from dragging. Alternating the exploration logs and the diary-type exposition was a good choice as well.

Two editing things to look at: there's a point where you use "Scantron" rather than Scranton in a character's dialogue, which I assume was an author mistake rather than an in-character mistake. Second, at one point the MTF lead clips "themselves" to a rope; typically for a single person you might use "themself" or "theirself."

Looking forward to reading more of your work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The suit is the final remains of Their [Majesty], They That Shall [Inherit] All.

It's the thematic crux of the entire piece. Nothing remains of this once great and arrogant monarch but the visage of their armour, reduced to just another number, and mistaken for a piece of rubble.

I included the poem this is based upon in my opening comment, which should help it make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this, the world building was amazing and as soon as you mentioned Alexandria Burning it made sense, I do agree with Erasmus though that it is too obscure to be found/realized without help. Overall +1

I am confused as to what the suit is though, could you or someone else shed some light on that please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore this. A compelling, if not particularly obscure, mystery, a competent foundation, a powerful message, and some very cool ideas. I was engaged throughout the whole piece, which considering the length is saying something. +1

Only real issue I have with it is the Alexandria connection is too tenuous and obscure. As is, it just seems like the civilization discrepancy is an unexplained aspect of the mystery that never gets developed. There is only payoff if the reader is deeply familiar with 4001 and you as an author, and even then it is easily missable without reading the comments here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ditto! And one more correction: "the initial internal exploration of SCP-5000-1" should probably be "SCP-5000-2"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my favorite thing about this is how the MTF's feel competent. So many skips fall into having MTF logs where the MTF's are just idiotic cannon fodder. They're teams trained by the foundation who specialize in a specific field and are experts in what they do. The MTFs in this entry feel very much like that. Prepared, cautious, and professional yet human. There's a lot I like about this entry but I wanted to point this out since I see it so rarely.

item.title: This was a captivating read...
item.content:encoded:
…so my nitpicks annoyed me even more than usual, because they've broken the flow. So i get the feeling i must drop my thoughts somewhere.

There are some tonal issues for example, which i don't particularly like. "Sunlight gently illuminates" (btw, you used "gently quite often") is to prose-y for my taste.

Stuff like explaining a cognitohazard-scanner in a footnote, but expecting people to know what a cog-filter is in the first place… i call it odd.

But hey, i enjoyed the Hector-bullying!

…overall, i get the feeling you missed the theme of the contest, and the whole story is hard on the verge of "still an SCP anomaly" — i'd rather expected this as a tale set in the Project Heimdal canon.
But in the end, it's still of higher quality than most other entries, so you get a solid novote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a few tiny corrections:

+ show block
– hide block

"P26-Lead: Thanks Command. We should be right for now"

do you mean alright?

"Due to their familiarity with the general structure and function of SCP-5000 in general"

I don't think you need two generals here.

" It's only about 20 feet down."
I thought SCP measurements were in meters?

" I have traveled farther from home than perhaps any other being, and seen many wonders other can only dream of."

I think you mean "others"

I'm feeling a little fatigued by super long 5k entries, I'm trying to read them all before the contest ends… but honestly this was an overall enjoyable read. I didn't get distracted or start sighing in exasperation as having to open up yet another considerable. I don't think I could say it better than TL. a simple concept told in an elegant and engaging way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe the captain is not a "him" or "her", though? I felt like the use of they/ them pronouns was pretty deliberate here, and I personally really liked it. otherwise, i agree. there are some errors here and there that a thorough proofreading could help with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's amazing how simple this is at its core. I mean, a crashed ancient alien spaceship? That's almost mundane compared to other things the Foundation has to contain and cover up.

But the way the story is told, both in its turns and in the formatting with all those approximated words, was excellent. That said, you will want to scan this again for errors, most of them were things a spellchecker wouldn't find, like Psi (I think) 3 talking to himself, or a "him" referring to the alien Captain. Given the length, the number of errors is within tolerances, so have another +1. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is such an excellent case of how to properly use collapsibles.

this is a proper cinematic piece. and it's HUGE. but it never feels like it's too big, or impenetrable, or confusing. it's properly paced, broken up into consumable chunks, and the presence of interesting characters and a gripping tragic tale make you WANT to keep going.

there's so much here that other 5000s are lacking. a +1 to you, and godspeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where this links to 4001 is in the disconnect between what the wayfarers expect to find at their destination. They find an uninhabited world, but Nine Solemn Wind first found the world (Earth) because of the presence of a thriving civilization: Homo nobilus, and is very concerned and confused by what their records tell them.

The events of Alexandria Burning and the subsequent reset happen while they're en route to Earth.

The lack of greater consequence and influence is sort of the thematic point. The entire piece is a massive 'fuck you' to its central antagonist, and tyranny and facism in general. It's very deliberate, from the poem from which this piece draws its name, to the ending where Their Majesty's corpse is originally mistaken as just another lump of shrapnel. Their legacy is just a broken wreck lost in the desert.

I do plan on further revising this to clean up parts I'm not satisfied with. There's a lot of untapped potential here and I was rushing to hit the deadline. E10 are deliberately clinical and professional, but there's room to properly develop the familial aspects of P26.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't quite follow where this links into Alexandria Eternal.

I had a few issues with this. I wasn't a fan of the exploration log sections - I found the MTF characters forgettable (after reading, I didn't retain any sense of who was who or what their personalities were like), and as a result just shrugged when one unfortunate guy got knocked off. There were also some instances of not very clinical descriptions and a few SPAG errors.

Unless I missed it, maybe with the Alexandria link, I also noticed there didn't seem to be much relevance of this huge ancient advanced spaceship to anything in the real world. That's not necessarily a problem - 'the ancient aliens did this thing' has been done a billion times - I was just expecting some bigger connection to the real world or Foundation lore to materialise that never came.

Having said all that, I still liked this. It has a neat classic sci-fi adventure feel, without being too derivative and incorporating some interesting themes about faith and loyalty. I think the core idea of an alien race that uses a cognitohazardous language is great - it's original, yet still sounds like something Clarke would have written about, and is simple and easy to understand while still being a distinctly SCP-flavoured thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everything about this one is absolutely amazing. I was already enjoying it from the start- I love extraterrestrial SCPs with a passion- but I was completely sold around the time I read about their language being made of visual cognitohazards. That was just so goddamn cool I actually went "wowww" out loud, and that's without going into the phenomenal story and mechanics of how the alien ships function. Outstanding work overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. One of the best SCPs i've ever read. It's taken me around an hour to read but for me it was powerful and gripping throughly from start to end. I love extraterrestrial SCPs and I thought this was a delightfully fresh take on the whole alien/UFO concept. The world building was done just right and was enough to really engage me and also get me thinking about the home and the people of these ships. This got me feeling all sorts of emotions throughout and I think it ended perfectly.

Did I mention I loved the characters too? Easy peasy +1 and I hope it wins

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I met a traveller from an antique land,
Who said—“Two vast and trunkless legs of stone
Stand in the desert… . Near them, on the sand,
Half sunk a shattered visage lies, whose frown,
And wrinkled lip, and sneer of cold command,
Tell that its sculptor well those passions read
Which yet survive, stamped on these lifeless things,
The hand that mocked them, and the heart that fed;
And on the pedestal, these words appear:
My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings;
Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!
Nothing beside remains. Round the decay
Of that colossal Wreck, boundless and bare
The lone and level sands stretch far away.”

This was a wild meandering ride, drawing on a multitude of inspirations. It tries to touch on themes of hubris and tyranny, and the defiance before these evils.

For the 4000 Contest, I presented Alexandria Eternal. It only seems fitting to expand upon that canon for the 5000 Contest.

It is as much an exercise in worldbuilding, as it is interested in being a mystery.

I can only hope that you appreciate it.

Requested slots in order: 5001, 5999, 5052, 5250, 5005.
